citations.narrative:
The following housing areas containing birds are in need of repair:
• Within the indoor wire primary enclosure of a cockatoo, wire is missing creating various sized gaps and holes with sharp
points. Per the licensee, the cockatoo created these gaps and holes. A lot of the wire used on the enclosure appears to
be smaller gauge, allowing the cockatoo to more easily damage them. The holes in the cockatoo's enclosure could entrap
the bird or a body part while the sharp points could cause an injury.
• At a wire crate within the outdoor poultry primary enclosure, a section of twisted chicken wire that had sharp points along
its edges was draped over the outside of the crate, leaving it accessible to the birds, since it hung over the crate, from
about 3 feet in height, to the ground. The sharp points could cause injury, and the twisted nature of the wire could
entangle a bird.
Housing facilities for birds that are not structurally sound or in good repair may cause harm, discomfort, or injury to the
birds. Housing facilities for birds must be designed and constructed so that they are structurally sound for the species of
bird housed in them. They must be kept in good repair, protect the birds from injury, and restrict other animals from
entering.

The current Record of Animals on hand (acquisition record and disposition records) is again not up to date or complete.
Since the last inspection, the following has occurred:
• Six sheep have been born at the facility and not recorded.
• Disposition of one rabbit was not recorded.
• The following animals are still not listed on the inventory: Five budgies, a cockatoo, three pheasants, four emus, four
sheep, and the quantity of chickens.
Accurate and complete acquisition and disposition records are necessary to allow for medical and legal tracking of the
animals. The facility shall ensure that it makes, keeps and maintains records which fully and correctly disclose the
following information concerning all exhibition animals: name and address of person from whom the animal was acquired,
their USDA license number or Driver's license number/state, the name and address of the disposition of the animal, the
date of acquisition and/or disposition, and the species of the animal. The records shall include any offspring born of any
animal while in his or her possession.

The current contingency plan still lacks specific tasks and details related to identified emergencies. For example,
evacuation is listed in inclement weather but no further details on how to contain and move all species from the facility are
written. Further, escaped animals, loss of electrical power, feed and water supply disruptions, and road closures are all
listed as potential emergencies without details on how to address those identified emergencies. A complete written
contingency plan is required to help ensure animal health and well-being in the event of an emergency or disaster; without
this tool, animals may suffer illness, injury, or death due to unpreparedness. Correct by developing, documenting and
ensuring the written contingency plan includes the specific tasks required to be carried out in response to the identified
emergencies to include such topics as sources of backup food and water as well as sanitation, ventilation, transport for all
species if evacuating, bedding, and veterinary care, etc. Finally, a chain of command needs to be identified while also
addressing how response and recovery will be handled in terms of materials, resources, and training needed.

There is still no documentation maintained to indicate that an annual review of the contingency plan has been completed
since 5/20/2024. The contingency plan must be reviewed by the facility on at least an annual basis to ensure that it
adequately addresses all required contingency-related criteria and that no updates or changes need to be made to the
plan such as changes made as a result of any recent unforeseen circumstances. The facility must maintain
documentation of their contingency plan annual reviews including documenting any amendments or changes made since
the previous year's review.

Within the primary outdoor enclosure for ten rabbits, one plastic hutch, a circular shelter (appeared as upside-down
bucket/planter approximately 2 feet in diameter and 12 inches high), had two large holes on the top and on one side
where it had cracked forming sharp corners. An identical plastic hutch, within the same enclosure, that was intact had one
rabbit sitting on its top. If a rabbit jumped up or sat on the top of this cracked hutch, the holes or cracks in the hutch could
entrap a rabbit’s body part or might not provide enough support to maintain the structural strength of the hutch. The hutch
must be repaired or replaced to protect the animals from injury.
Correct by: 04/01/2026.

Ten rabbits are housed in an outdoor open-topped wire enclosure on the ground that does not provide sufficient shelter
from rain for all rabbits at one time. Two hutches, both circular shelters (appeared as an upside-down buckets/planters
approximately 2 feet in diameter) are present but one had a large hole encompassing the top and one side, therefore, it
was not a functional shelter. A third hutch, a plastic outdoor rectangular doghouse (approximately 30 inches long by 18
inches wide), is provided as well as pop-up fabric tunnel designed for indoor use (approximately 10 inches in diameter
and 48 inches long with a secondary connecting "T" tunnel of approximately 12 inches). The fabric tunnel was not made
from waterproof material and was uniformly wet during the inspection even though it was dry weather conditions,
therefore, it was not made of adequate material to keep the animals dry. Therefore, only two functional hutches were
provided, one upside down bucket and one doghouse, but even considering them together, they were insufficient in size
to allow all ten rabbits to shelter within them and remain dry during rain. Not providing rabbits with access to sufficient
shelter during rain could lead to illness as well as animal discomfort. Rabbits kept outdoors shall be provided with access
to shelter to allow them to remain dry during rain or snow.

Ten rabbits are housed in an outdoor open-topped wire enclosure on the ground that does not provide sufficient shelter
for all the rabbits when the temperature was below 40 degrees Fahrenheit at the time of inspection. During the inspection,
the temperature was 35.8° Fahrenheit (measured via Kestrel) and the low overnight was in the 20s (per Wunderground)
with a freeze warning issued the night before and after this inspection. There was not enough space for all ten rabbits to
shelter from the cold due to the inadequate shelters provided, and even more so considering the floor of all the hutches
provided was either wet mud or mud mixed with excrement without bedding. Two hutches, both circular shelters
(appeared as an upside-down buckets/planters approximately 2 feet in diameter) are present but one had a large hole on
the top and one side, therefore, not allowing it to be a functional shelter. A third hutch, a plastic outdoor rectangular
doghouse (approximately 30 inches long by 18 inches wide), is provided with a pop-up fabric tunnel designed for indoor
use (approximately 10 inches in diameter and 48 inches long with a secondary connecting "T" tunnel of approximately 12
inches). The fabric tunnel was not made from adequate material to protect the rabbits from the cold and was also found
uniformly wet during the inspection even though it was dry weather conditions. So, only two functional hutches were
provided, one upside down bucket and one doghouse, but even together, they were insufficient in size to allow all ten
rabbits to shelter at one time when the temperature was below 40 degrees. Low temperatures such as these could result
in animal disease or discomfort. Shelter must be provided for all rabbits kept outdoors when the atmospheric temperature
falls below 40°F.
Correct by: 04/01/2026.

At the facility, during the inspection, within the rabbits outdoor open-topped wire enclosure, the dirt ground is not
adequately draining. Despite dry weather conditions at the time of inspection, the enclosure was muddy with some
puddles and most of the rabbits’ feet were wet. Some rabbits with wet feet were observed flicking their feet in the air and
then grooming them. When the inspector walked within the enclosure, water would rise to pool up around their boots in
most areas. Two rabbits were observed sitting on top of provided shelters instead of within them. The excess water in the
enclosure presents a hazard to the animal as the rabbits may become excessively wet which could result in negative
health issues, especially combined with the cold temperatures experienced during this inspection, 35.8 degrees
Fahrenheit (measured via Kestrel) with freeze warnings issued the night before and after this inspection. Methods should
be employed by the facility to ensure that excess water is able to be rapidly eliminated from the enclosure to prevent
excessive accumulation.
Correct by: 04/01/2026.

During inspection, the produce fed to the rabbits in their outdoor primary enclosure was found on the wet, muddy ground,
contaminated with accumulated excreta and not within a food receptacle. When asked, the licensee stated he scatters the
produce for the rabbits to eat on the ground and does not place it in a receptacle. Contamination of feed by excreta can
lead to the spread of disease, inappetence, or poor body condition. The food provided shall be free from contamination,
wholesome, palatable and of sufficient quantity and nutritive value to meet the meet the normal daily requirements for the
condition and size of the rabbits.
Correct by: 04/01/2026.

The shelters within the outdoor primary enclosure and the ground where the ten rabbits are fed need to be cleaned. Of
the three shelters provided, two upside-down plastic buckets/planters (about 2 feet in diameter) and a plastic doghouse
(approximately 30 inches long x 18 inches wide), were both dirty inside and out. Within the third shelter, a plastic
doghouse, there was an accumulation of dirt and dark staining along the top, green material and dirt were observed
uniformly across most of the front exterior wall with dirt across the bottom third of the front wall. Also, mud and dirt were
observed extending up the sides of the interior walls within the doghouse shelter, and excreta and mud had built up
covering the plastic floor. The upside-down plastic buckets/planter shelters had mud/dirt floors with dirt extending up the
walls within. Also, the rabbits’ food was provided in one corner of the enclosure where an accumulation of excrement had
collected such that the rabbits must walk through the excrement that covers a majority of the area, in order to eat their
provided feed. Accumulation of excreta and lack of clean bedding/shelters can lead to contamination of the animals,
odors, and possible spread of disease. Primary enclosures, and the shelters within, housing rabbits must be kept
reasonably free of excreta and other debris by appropriate periodic cleaning.

The perimeter fence around the sheltered enclosure for five ring tail lemurs was insufficient again in many areas. The
perimeter fence was constructed of 6-foot-tall wire mesh secured by 6-foot-tall metal T posts that, per the licensee, his
camel in the pasture surrounding this enclosure pushes and leans on, which has created multiple bowed in areas towards
the lemur’s outdoor portion of their sheltered enclosure. The distance from the primary enclosure varied but was only 27
inches in one area. The height also varied but reached a low of 54 inches to 67 inches in many areas due to the fence
being pushed in and/or down along three sides of the perimeter fence, excluding the back side facing the neighboring
property. Also, by the access gate into the lemur's outdoor portion of their enclosure, the wire mesh of the fence has been
stretched and bent such that large gaps were created between the wires, approximately 7.5 inches high from the ground x
9-10 inches wide as observed starting at the bottom of the fence to approximately 2 feet up from the ground. The lack of
an adequate perimeter fence presents a safety risk to the animals within the facility due to an inadequate secondary
containment system to hold animals that may escape their primary enclosures, or a sufficient barrier to keep animals and
unauthorized persons out. The facility must ensure that the nonhuman primates being housed in enclosures that are
enclosed by an adequate perimeter fence, 6-feet tall, that is constructed so that it restricts unauthorized humans, and
animals the size of dogs, skunks, and raccoons from going through it or under it and having contact with the nonhuman
primates. The perimeter fence must be effective, continuous, and at least 3 feet in distance from the primary enclosures
unless a variance is obtained and approved by the Administrator.

In the second pasture accessible to the goats, sheep, alpacas, and camel, the larger wooden shelter (approximately 4
feet tall) was again in disrepair as below:
• Two wooden sideboards on the right side were still missing with one fallen board still lying on the ground.
• The metal roof on top of the shelter was bent and beginning to lift off the wooden sides creating gaps.
The areas identified above could cause harm to the animals by allowing injury to a body part by getting stuck in between
the gaps in the boards on the sides of the shelter or at the roof. The outdoor housing facilities, including shelters, must be
structurally sound and maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury or escape.

Within the 3-sided barn, three covered stalls provided as shelter for the pastured animals (camel, goats, alpacas, and
sheep which is also shared with horses, ponies, and donkeys), the ground within the stalls had a thick layer of mud with
many deep hoofprints and some puddles of water present. Only one dry triangular corner area, approximately 4 x 4 x 6
feet, at the back of the left stall was present, which cannot accommodate all the animals at one time, either standing or
lying down. During the inspection, the temperature was 35.8° Fahrenheit (measured via Kestrel) and the low overnight
was in the 20s (per Wunderground) with a freeze warning issued the night before and after this inspection. Also, not only
was this shelter the only shelter tall enough for the most of the species in the pasture to enter and share (camel, alpacas,
horses, donkeys, and ponies) to get out of the cold, but it also required them to walk through even thicker and deeper
mud, including standing water, to gain access to the shelter due to the lack of drainage in this area overall. The
inspector's boots sunk about 2-3 inches into mud in many areas and completely sank under the mud in others. A goat was
observed attempting to walk around the deepest muddy areas to gain access to the area where the grain food bucket
receptacles were placed. Most of the animals in this pasture had muddy hooves and lower legs. Enclosures that do not
drain effectively and in which the ground is muddy with no dry path to or within the shelter, can cause animals to develop
hoof or skin issues due to excessive moisture and mud exposure. Correct by ensuring that a suitable method is provided
to rapidly eliminate excessive water within the animals' shelter and ensure that the animals can access their shelter as
well as their feed and water without walking through mud.
Correct by: 04/15/2026.

The emus’ outdoor enclosure has inadequate drainage since it has standing water and excessively wet and muddy at an
area of their enclosure that is their only access to shelter. The mud is so wet in this area, the birds' feet were sinking into
the mud causing their feet to be wet and muddy. The emus must go through this wet muddy area to get to their only
provided shelter. The inadequate drainage can lead to discomfort, decreased body temperature regulation and potential
spread of disease. Housing facilities for birds must have drainage systems that are constructed and maintained so that
water is rapidly eliminated and animals can remain dry.
Correct by: 04/15/2026.

Five emus in their primary outdoor enclosure still did not have adequate shelter provided to accommodate all five birds at
one time from inclement weather. The only shelter provided was a three-sided wooden structure, partially roofed, making
the under-roof area approximately 6 x 6 x 6 feet high. Two large plastic trash bins and another large bucket are also
stored within this roofed structure along the left side which decreases the amount of floor space available for the birds to
approximately 4 x 6 x 6 feet high. Providing shelter from inclement weather, that is accessible and can hold all the birds, is
necessary to provide for their comfort, protection, and physical well-being. Outdoor housing facilities must provide
adequate shelter for the local climatic conditions including direct sunlight, precipitation, and wind. Shelter must also be
constructed to provide sufficient space to comfortably hold all of the birds at the same time, including birds that are low in
the hierarchy, without adverse intraspecific aggression or grouping of incompatible birds.

citations.desc: Environment enhancement to promote psychological well-being.
citations.narrative:
The facility was unable to provide the inspector with a written plan of environmental enhancement for their birds. Facilities
that house birds must develop, document, and follow a species-appropriate plan for environment enhancement adequate
to promote the psychological well-being of birds. The licensee must develop, document, and follow a species-appropriate
plan that has been approved by the attending veterinarian. The plan must be in accordance with AWA regulations and
with currently accepted professional standards as cited in appropriate professional journals or reference guides.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
The indoor primary enclosure housing four rose-ringed parakeets and the outdoor poultry enclosure are in need of
cleaning. In the rose-ringed parakeets primary enclosure, excess accumulations of excreta have continued to collect on a
wooden platform, and the horizontal wood beams that provide part of the frame for this wire enclosure. These horizontal
surfaces were mostly covered in excrement that has dried and accumulated. In the outdoor poultry enclosure housing
chickens, peafowl, and pheasants, an excessive amount of excreta has accumulated on the perches and wood surfaces
in the roosting shelter around the provided nest boxes. Regular cleaning is necessary to prevent soiling of the birds and to
reduce disease hazards, insects, pests, and odors. Excreta, debris, and food waste shall be removed from primary
enclosures and from under and around primary enclosures as often as necessary to prevent excessive accumulations.
This is a continuation of the report for the routine inspection conducted 03/17/2027 and contains the remaining non-
compliances found during the inspection. The exit briefing for the items included on this report was conducted on
03/24/2026.
This inspection was conducted with the licensee on 03/17/2026 and continued until 03/20/2026. The preliminary exit
interview was conducted with the licensee on 03/17/2026.
n

the mud causing their feet to be wet and muddy. The emus must go through this wet muddy area to get to their only 
provided shelter. The inadequate drainage can lead to discomfort, decreased body temperature regulation and potential 
spread of disease. Housing facilities for birds must have drainage systems that are constructed and maintained so that 
water is rapidly eliminated and animals can remain dry. 
Correct by: 04/15/2026. 
3.152(b)  Repeat

Facilities, outdoor 
Five emus in their primary outdoor enclosure still did not have adequate shelter provided to accommodate all five birds at 
one time from inclement weather. The only shelter provided was a three-sided wooden structure, partially roofed, making 
the under-roof area approximately 6 x 6 x 6 feet high. Two large plastic trash bins and another large bucket are also 
stored within this roofed structure along the left side which decreases the amount of floor space available for the birds to 
approximately 4 x 6 x 6 feet high. Providing shelter from inclement weather, that is accessible and can hold all the birds, is 
necessary to provide for their comfort, protection, and physical well-being. Outdoor housing facilities must provide 
adequate shelter for the local climatic conditions including direct sunlight, precipitation, and wind. Shelter must also be 
constructed to provide sufficient space to comfortably hold all of the birds at the same time, including birds that are low in 
the hierarchy, without adverse intraspecific aggression or grouping of incompatible birds. 
3.154  Repeat
   
Environment enhancement to promote psychological well-being. 
The facility was unable to provide the inspector with a written plan of environmental enhancement for their birds. Facilities 
that house birds must develop, document, and follow a species-appropriate plan for environment enhancement adequate 
to promote the psychological well-being of birds. The licensee must develop, document, and follow a species-appropriate 
plan that has been approved by the attending veterinarian. The plan must be in accordance with AWA regulations and 
with currently accepted professional standards as cited in appropriate professional journals or reference guides. 
3.158(a)(1)  Repeat
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
The indoor primary enclosure housing four rose-ringed parakeets and the outdoor poultry enclosure are in need of 
cleaning. In the rose-ringed parakeets primary enclosure, excess accumulations of excreta have continued to collect on a 
wooden platform, and the horizontal wood beams that provide part of the frame for this wire enclosure. These horizontal 
surfaces were mostly covered in excrement that has dried and accumulated. In the outdoor poultry enclosure housing 
chickens, peafowl, and pheasants, an excessive amount of excreta has accumulated on the perches and wood surfaces 
in the roosting shelter around the provided nest boxes. Regular cleaning is necessary to prevent soiling of the birds and to 
reduce disease hazards, insects, pests, and odors. Excreta, debris, and food waste shall be removed from primary 
enclosures and from under and around primary enclosures as often as necessary to prevent excessive accumulations. 
 
This is a continuation of the report for the routine inspection conducted 03/17/2027 and contains the remaining non-
compliances found during the inspection. The exit briefing for the items included on this report was conducted on 
03/24/2026. 
 
This inspection was conducted with the licensee on 03/17/2026 and continued until 03/20/2026. The preliminary exit 
interview was conducted with the licensee on 03/17/2026.
